content.body: Waves transfer energy without transferring matter. When two waves meet, they superimpose. Constructive interference increases amplitude; destructive reduces it.

Wave speed: v = fλ
Period: T = 1/f

Constructive interference: path difference = nλ
Destructive interference: path difference = (n+½)λ

Young's fringes: fringe spacing y = λD/d

content.title: Young's Double Slit
content.body:
λ = 600nm, D = 1.5m, d = 0.3mm
Fringe spacing y = λD/d
y = (600×10⁻⁹ × 1.5) / (0.3×10⁻³)
y = 3×10⁻³ m = 3mm

questions.question: A wave has frequency 200 Hz and wavelength 1.5m. Its speed is:
Constructive interference occurs when path difference is:
questions.explanation: Whole-number wavelength path difference gives constructive interference

questions.question: Increasing the slit separation d in Young's experiment:
questions.explanation: y = λD/d — fringe spacing decreases as d increases

questions.question: What type of waves can show polarisation?
questions.explanation: Polarisation only occurs in transverse waves (e.g. light, not sound)

questions.question: In a stationary wave, nodes are points of:
questions.explanation: Nodes are fixed points of zero displacement in a standing wave

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

content.body:
Wave equation: v = fλ
v = wave speed (m/s), f = frequency (Hz), λ = wavelength (m)

Period T = 1/f
Amplitude A = maximum displacement
Intensity ∝ A²

Superposition principle:
When waves meet, resultant displacement = sum of individual displacements

Constructive interference: waves in phase → amplitude doubles
Destructive interference: waves out of phase (180°) → cancel out
Path difference for constructive: nλ (n=0,1,2...)
Path difference for destructive: (n+½)λ

content.title: Young's Double Slit
content.body:
Fringe spacing: y = λD/d
λ = wavelength, D = distance to screen, d = slit separation
Used to measure wavelength of light
Light is a wave — it diffracts and interferes

questions.question: v = fλ relates wave speed to...
questions.explanation: Wave equation: speed = frequency × wavelength

questions.question: Frequency = 500 Hz, wavelength = 0.68 m. Wave speed =
questions.explanation: v = fλ = 500×0.68 = 340 m/s (speed of sound in air)

questions.question: Constructive interference occurs when path difference is...
questions.explanation: Constructive interference: path difference = 0, λ, 2λ, 3λ... (whole number of wavelengths, waves arrive in phase)

questions.question: Intensity of a wave is proportional to...
questions.explanation: Intensity ∝ A². Doubling the amplitude increases intensity by 4 times.

questions.question: In Young's double slit, fringe spacing increases if...
questions.explanation: y = λD/d. Increasing D (distance to screen) increases fringe spacing y.

questions.question: Destructive interference requires path difference of...
questions.explanation: Destructive interference: path difference = λ/2, 3λ/2, 5λ/2... — waves arrive half a wavelength out of phase.

questions.question: Period T and frequency f are related by...
questions.explanation: Period and frequency are reciprocals: T = 1/f and f = 1/T.

questions.question: Light through a double slit produces...
options: An interference pattern of bright and dark fringes
questions.answer: An interference pattern of bright and dark fringes
questions.explanation: Light diffracts through both slits and the two waves interfere, creating alternating bright (constructive) and dark (destructive) fringes.

questions.question: Superposition states that when waves meet...
options: Resultant displacement is sum of individual displacements
questions.answer: Resultant displacement is sum of individual displacements
questions.explanation: The principle of superposition: at any point, the total displacement equals the algebraic sum of all individual wave displacements.

questions.question: Doubling the amplitude of a wave multiplies intensity by...
questions.explanation: I ∝ A². Doubling A: new I ∝ (2A)² = 4A². Intensity multiplied by 4.

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

content.body:
Lens formula: 1/f = 1/v − 1/u
f = focal length, v = image distance, u = object distance
(Real is positive convention)

Magnification: m = v/u = image height/object height
Power of lens: P = 1/f (diopters, f in metres)

Converging lens (convex): f > 0
Diverging lens (concave): f < 0

content.title: Lens Calculation
content.body:
Object 30 cm from converging lens (f=10 cm). Find image.
1/v − 1/u = 1/f → 1/v = 1/10 + 1/(−30) = 3/30−1/30 = 2/30
v = 15 cm (real image, same side as outgoing light)
m = v/u = 15/30 = 0.5 (image is half the size)

content.title: Eye Defects
content.body:
Myopia (short sight): distant objects blurry. Eyeball too long. Corrected with diverging lens.
Hyperopia (long sight): close objects blurry. Eyeball too short. Corrected with converging lens.
Astigmatism: cornea not perfectly spherical. Corrected with cylindrical lens.

questions.question: The lens formula is...
questions.explanation: The thin lens formula relating focal length f, image distance v, and object distance u.

questions.question: Power of a lens with f = 0.25 m is...
questions.explanation: Power P = 1/f = 1/0.25 = 4 diopters (D)

questions.question: A converging lens has...
questions.explanation: A converging (convex) lens brings parallel rays to a focus, giving a positive focal length.

questions.question: Magnification m = 3 means...
questions.explanation: Magnification m = image size/object size. m=3 means image is 3 times larger than the object.

questions.question: Short-sightedness (myopia) is corrected with...
questions.explanation: Myopia: eyeball too long, image forms in front of retina. Diverging lens spreads rays before they enter the eye.

questions.question: Object 20cm from lens with f=10cm. Image distance v =
A virtual image formed by a lens...
questions.explanation: A virtual image cannot be projected — light rays only appear to come from that point but don't actually pass through it.

questions.question: Long-sightedness (hyperopia) means...
questions.explanation: Hyperopia: eyeball too short, image would form behind retina. Converging lens helps focus close objects.

questions.question: For a diverging lens, focal length is...
questions.explanation: Diverging (concave) lenses have negative focal lengths — they spread light rays apart.

questions.question: A camera uses a converging lens to form...
questions.explanation: A camera lens forms a real inverted image on the sensor/film. The image is real because light actually converges there.

Learning Objectives

By the end of this lesson you should be able to: (1) Apply Waves & Optics concepts to complex problems. (2) Use appropriate formulas and methods. (3) Interpret results in context. (4) Connect this topic to related areas of physics.
Core Theory

This section of Waves & Optics builds on foundational knowledge. In physics, advanced problem-solving requires not just knowing formulas but understanding when and how to apply them. Study each worked example carefully.

content.title: Worked Example 4.1
content.body: Consider a more complex problem on Waves & Optics. Approach: (1) Read and understand the problem. (2) Identify all given information. (3) Determine which concepts apply. (4) Solve systematically, showing all steps. (5) Check your answer is reasonable.

content.title: Worked Example 4.2

questions.question: What is the SI unit of force?
questions.explanation: Force is measured in Newtons (N). Named after Sir Isaac Newton. 1 N = 1 kg⋅m/s².

questions.question: State Newton's Second Law of Motion.
options: An object at rest stays at rest
Every action has an equal reaction
questions.explanation: Newton's Second Law: F = ma, where F is net force, m is mass, and a is acceleration.

questions.question: What is the speed if distance = 60 m and time = 12 s?
questions.explanation: Speed = distance ÷ time = 60 ÷ 12 = 5 m/s.

questions.question: What type of wave is light?
questions.explanation: Light is a transverse electromagnetic wave — particles oscillate perpendicular to the direction of travel.

questions.question: What is the unit of electrical resistance?
questions.explanation: Resistance is measured in Ohms (Ω), named after Georg Simon Ohm.

questions.question: Using V = IR, if I = 3A and R = 4Ω, find V.
What is the acceleration due to gravity on Earth?
questions.explanation: Gravitational acceleration on Earth's surface g ≈ 9.8 m/s² (often approximated as 10 m/s² in calculations).

questions.question: What is kinetic energy?
questions.explanation: Kinetic energy = ½mv², where m is mass and v is velocity. It is the energy possessed by a moving object.

questions.question: What happens to resistance as temperature increases (for most conductors)?
questions.explanation: For most metallic conductors, as temperature increases, atomic vibrations increase, impeding electron flow, so resistance increases.

questions.question: What is the frequency if period T = 0.5 s?
questions.explanation: Frequency f = 1/T = 1/0.5 = 2 Hz.

questions.question: Which quantity is a vector?
questions.explanation: Velocity is a vector quantity — it has both magnitude and direction. Speed is a scalar (magnitude only).

questions.question: What is the principle of conservation of energy?
options: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
Energy is always lost as heat
questions.answer: Energy cannot be created or destroyed, only transformed
questions.explanation: The law of conservation of energy states that the total energy in a closed system remains constant.

questions.question: What is the unit of power?
questions.explanation: Power is measured in Watts (W). 1 W = 1 J/s. Power is the rate of energy transfer.

questions.question: What is the boiling point of water at standard pressure?
questions.explanation: Water boils at 100°C (373 K) at standard atmospheric pressure (101.325 kPa).

questions.question: In a series circuit, the current:
options: Is the same through all components
Is largest at the first component
Is zero if one component fails
questions.answer: Is the same through all components
questions.explanation: In a series circuit, there is only one path for current, so the same current flows through all components.

questions.question: What is pressure?
questions.explanation: Pressure P = F/A, where F is force applied perpendicular to a surface area A. SI unit is Pascal (Pa).

questions.question: Which type of radiation has the highest penetrating power?
questions.explanation: Gamma rays are electromagnetic radiation with very short wavelength and highest penetrating power, requiring thick lead or concrete to stop.

questions.question: What happens to light when it travels from air into glass?
questions.explanation: Light slows down when entering a denser medium (glass) and changes direction — this is refraction.

questions.question: State the law of conservation of momentum.
options: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.answer: Total momentum before = total momentum after in a closed system
questions.explanation: In a closed system with no external forces, the total momentum before a collision equals the total momentum after.

questions.question: What is the half-life of a radioactive substance?
options: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
The time for all nuclei to decay
The time to double in activity
questions.answer: The time for half the radioactive nuclei to decay
questions.explanation: Half-life is the time taken for the activity (or number of radioactive nuclei) to decrease to half its original value.
